'''Toyama Shrine''' (十山神社, ''Tōyama Jinja'') is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in the {{ill|Sonai (Yonaguni)|ja|祖納|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonai (Yonaguni)|1=Sonai (Yonaguni)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588548}} district of [[:en:Yonaguni, Okinawa|Yonaguni, Okinawa]], [[:en:Yaeyama District, Okinawa|Yaeyama District, Okinawa]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan.

It is the westernmost shrine in Japan. The shrine is also known by its alternative name, '''Toyama Utaki''' (十山御嶽, ''Toyama Ugan'').

This shrine represents a unique fusion of [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] and the [[:en:Ontake|Utaki]] faith of the {{ill|Yaeyama Islands|ja|八重山諸島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaeyama Islands|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八重山列島|1=Yaeyama Islands|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q696496}}, maintaining distinctive traditions. The shrine’s annual {{ill|豊年祭|ja|豊年祭|1=Hōnensai|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/豊年祭|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōnensai|qq=}} (Harvest Festival) is held on {{ill|June 26 (Lunisolar)|lt=June 26 (Lunisolar)|lt=June 26 (lunar calendar)|ja|6月26日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 26 (lunar calendar)|qq=}} and includes a sacred [[:en:Tug of war|Tug-of-war]] ritual.
==Excerpt from [[History of Shinto shrines in Okinawa]]==

=== Shrines during the war and the one village, one shrine concept ===
[[File:Miyako_harimizu_utaki.jpg|thumb|When {{ill|Miyako Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=漲水御嶽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyako Shrine|qq=}} was relocated to its current location in 1940, a combined ceremony was held and the shrine came to be called Hashimizu Shrine. {{Sfn|大城直樹|2022}}]]
During the same period, shrines closely related to war were also built. After the outbreak of {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|8=en|lt=the Russo-Japanese War|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Russo-Japanese War|qq=}} in 1904, prayers for victory began to be held repeatedly at Naminoue Shrine, and a ceremony to declare war on Japan in {{ill|World War I|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World War I|qq=}} was held there in 1914, and a ceremony to declare entry into the military was held there in 1917. In this situation, there was a demand for a shrine to commemorate those [[:en:Killed in action|Killed in action]], but the area surrounding [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]] was already a bustling shopping district, and it was recognized that it was not suitable as a place to worship the war dead. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} A new shrine was built in 1936 (Showa 11), and the shrine was renamed {{ill|Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=沖縄県護国神社|lt=Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} in 1940 (Showa 15). At the time of the name change, the shrine enshrined 310 deities in total, including one from the Sino-Japanese War and 195 from the Russo-Japanese War, making it a small shrine compared to [[:en:Gokoku shrines|the Gokoku Shrines]] of other prefectures. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}

Okinawa is characterized by the fact that Gokoku Shrines dedicated to war dead were built by private citizens, and as of 2018, some still exist within the U.S. {{ill|Kadena Air Base|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadena Air Base|qq=}} and on [[:en:Miyako Island|Miyako Island]] . These are thought to have been enshrined at the Utaki shrine, and the rituals there are performed in the style of Ryukyu Shinto. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}

Also, since there were no infantry regiments in Okinawa Prefecture, being conscripted was the same as being sent to war outside the prefecture. It was known in Okinawa that on the mainland prayers for the military success of soldiers were offered at shrines, but in Okinawa in the early Showa period, there were only four functioning shrines with priests: [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]], {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, [[Okinawa Shrine]], and {{ill|Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=沖縄県護国神社|lt=Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}. This raised the question of what to do in areas without shrines. As a result, six of the {{ill|Eight Ryukyu Shrines|ja|琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}}, which had fallen into disrepair, were quickly upgraded to prefectural or local shrines{{Efn|The six shrines are
* {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū|ja|安里八幡宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asato-Hachimangū|qq=}}
* {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}
* {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}
* {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}
* {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}
* {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}}}, and a plan was proposed to develop the local utaki shrines into shrines. This idea was first proposed around 1939, and was featured in a newspaper article on August 27, 1940 (Showa 15) as "One Village, One Company" {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} .The one village, one shrine concept was initially shelved due to lack of funds, but in 1943 the prefecture applied to the national government for grants to restore the [[eight Ryukyu shrines]]. In addition, five new shrines were designated as prefectural shrines {{Refn|{{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sefa-utaki|lt=Saiba Shrine|ja|斎場神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sefa-utaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ([[:en:Sefa-utaki|Sefa-utaki]]), {{ill|Nakijin Castle|lt=Hokuzan Shrine|ja|北山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakijin Castle|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ([[:en:Nakijin Castle|Nakijin Castle]]), {{ill|Miyako Shrine|ja|宮古神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyako Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Yaeyama Shrine|ja|八重山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaeyama Shrine|1=Yaeyama Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391791}} (possibly {{ill|Ōishigaki Utaki|ja|大石垣御嶽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōishigaki Utaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}){{sfn|後田多敦ほか|2020|pp=25-28}} make up the five shrines{{sfn|加治順人|2018|pp=53-55}}.|group=Note}}, and a plan was drawn up to consolidate the more than 900 Utaki shrines into 60 village shrines and 150 subordinate shrines. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} Among the utaki that have been converted into shrines are {{ill|Shirogane-do|8=en|10=ja|11=白銀堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirogane-do|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Itoman, Okinawa|Itoman]], {{ill|Tsukishiro-gu Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=月代宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukishiro-gu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Nanjō|Sashiki]], [[:en:Sashiki, Okinawa|Nanjō]],  {{ill|Sōkei-gu Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=惣慶宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōkei-gu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Awase Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=泡瀬神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awase Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Ginoza, Okinawa|Ginoza village]], {{ill|Shinzato-gu Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=新里宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinzato-gu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Motobu, Okinawa|Motobu, Okinawa]],  {{ill|Toyama Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=十山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyama Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Yonaguni, Okinawa|Yonaguni town]], and {{ill|Shirahama-jinja Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=白浜神社 (竹富町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirahama-jinja Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on {{ill|Iriomote Island|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iriomote Island|qq=}} . {{Sfn|加治順人|2018|qq=draft}} In addition, training seminars for [[:en:Noro (priestess)|Noro]] recommended by local government leaders were held at Gokoku Shrines and other locations in an attempt to fill the shortage of priests by switching them from Noro to other priests. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} {{Refn|In contrast, [[:en:Yuta (shaman)|Yuta]] were being targeted for regulation during the same period for "misleading the public."{{sfn|加治順人|2018|pp=55-57}}。|group=Note}}
 However, the four-year plan, one village, one company, was thwarted by the worsening war situation. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} Furthermore, the Ministry of Home Affairs documents state that there were ten shrines in Okinawa as of 1938 (Showa 13) {{Sfn|影山正治|1972}}, but Sueyasu Daiko (1992) claims that by the end of the war the number of shrines was as high as 227 {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} .
Many shrines were destroyed and fell into disrepair following {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|the Battle of Okinawa|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Okinawa|qq=}} . However, it is not clear whether all of these were the result of U.S. military attacks. For example, in photographs taken by the U.S. military immediately after the Battle of Okinawa, Gokoku Shrine was still standing, although there was a large hole in the roof of the worship hall. However, Nagamine Ushio (1953) {{Refn|"The Past, Present, and Future of Gokoku Shrine," The Journey of Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine, pp. 119-128 (originally published in 1953)
|group=Note}}
 {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}
testified that "when I saw it in 1946, the torii gate was still there, but a few years later it was gone without a trace," suggesting that it may have been dismantled and used as building materials during the postwar reconstruction period.

'''Toyama Shrine''' (十山神社, ''Tōyama Jinja'') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in the {{ill|Sonai (Yonaguni)|ja|祖納|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonai (Yonaguni)|1=Sonai (Yonaguni)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588548}} district of {{ill|Yonaguni, Okinawa|en|Yonaguni, Okinawa|WD=Q702623|lt=Yonaguni, Okinawa}}, {{ill|Yaeyama District, Okinawa|en|Yaeyama District, Okinawa|WD=Q629725|lt=Yaeyama District, Okinawa}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}}, Japan.

It is the westernmost shrine in Japan. The shrine is also known by its alternative name, '''Toyama Utaki''' (十山御嶽, ''Toyama Ugan'').

This shrine represents a unique fusion of {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|WD=Q812767|lt=Shinto}} and the {{ill|Ontake|en|Ontake|WD=Q11071176|lt=Utaki}} faith of the {{ill|Yaeyama Islands|ja|八重山諸島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaeyama Islands|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八重山列島|1=Yaeyama Islands|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q696496}}, maintaining distinctive traditions. The shrine’s annual {{ill|豊年祭|ja|豊年祭|1=Hōnensai|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/豊年祭|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōnensai|qq=}} (Harvest Festival) is held on {{ill|June 26 (Lunisolar)|lt=June 26 (Lunisolar)|lt=June 26 (lunar calendar)|ja|6月26日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 26 (lunar calendar)|qq=}} and includes a sacred {{ill|Tug of war|en|Tug of war|WD=Q102843|lt=Tug-of-war}} ritual.
==Excerpt from [[History of Shinto shrines in Okinawa]]==

=== Shrines during the war and the one village, one shrine concept ===
[[File:Miyako_harimizu_utaki.jpg|thumb|When {{ill|Miyako Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=漲水御嶽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyako Shrine|qq=}} was relocated to its current location in 1940, a combined ceremony was held and the shrine came to be called Hashimizu Shrine. {{Sfn|大城直樹|2022}}]]
During the same period, shrines closely related to war were also built. After the outbreak of {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|8=en|lt=the Russo-Japanese War|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Russo-Japanese War|qq=}} in 1904, prayers for victory began to be held repeatedly at Naminoue Shrine, and a ceremony to declare war on Japan in {{ill|World War I|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World War I|qq=}} was held there in 1914, and a ceremony to declare entry into the military was held there in 1917. In this situation, there was a demand for a shrine to commemorate those {{ill|Killed in action|en|Killed in action|WD=Q210392|lt=Killed in action}}, but the area surrounding {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|WD=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine}} was already a bustling shopping district, and it was recognized that it was not suitable as a place to worship the war dead. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} A new shrine was built in 1936 (Showa 11), and the shrine was renamed {{ill|Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=沖縄県護国神社|lt=Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} in 1940 (Showa 15). At the time of the name change, the shrine enshrined 310 deities in total, including one from the Sino-Japanese War and 195 from the Russo-Japanese War, making it a small shrine compared to {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=the Gokoku Shrines}} of other prefectures. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}

Okinawa is characterized by the fact that Gokoku Shrines dedicated to war dead were built by private citizens, and as of 2018, some still exist within the U.S. {{ill|Kadena Air Base|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadena Air Base|qq=}} and on {{ill|Miyako Island|en|Miyako Island|WD=Q700946|lt=Miyako Island}} . These are thought to have been enshrined at the Utaki shrine, and the rituals there are performed in the style of Ryukyu Shinto. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}

Also, since there were no infantry regiments in Okinawa Prefecture, being conscripted was the same as being sent to war outside the prefecture. It was known in Okinawa that on the mainland prayers for the military success of soldiers were offered at shrines, but in Okinawa in the early Showa period, there were only four functioning shrines with priests: {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|WD=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, [[Okinawa Shrine]], and {{ill|Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=沖縄県護国神社|lt=Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}. This raised the question of what to do in areas without shrines. As a result, six of the {{ill|Eight Ryukyu Shrines|ja|琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}}, which had fallen into disrepair, were quickly upgraded to prefectural or local shrines{{Efn|The six shrines are
* {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū|ja|安里八幡宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asato-Hachimangū|qq=}}
* {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}
* {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}
* {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}
* {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}
* {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}}}, and a plan was proposed to develop the local utaki shrines into shrines. This idea was first proposed around 1939, and was featured in a newspaper article on August 27, 1940 (Showa 15) as "One Village, One Company" {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} .The one village, one shrine concept was initially shelved due to lack of funds, but in 1943 the prefecture applied to the national government for grants to restore the [[eight Ryukyu shrines]]. In addition, five new shrines were designated as prefectural shrines {{Refn|{{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sefa-utaki|lt=Saiba Shrine|ja|斎場神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sefa-utaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Sefa-utaki|en|Sefa-utaki|WD=Q3087076|lt=Sefa-utaki}}), {{ill|Nakijin Castle|lt=Hokuzan Shrine|ja|北山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakijin Castle|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Nakijin Castle|en|Nakijin Castle|WD=Q508866|lt=Nakijin Castle}}), {{ill|Miyako Shrine|ja|宮古神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyako Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Yaeyama Shrine|ja|八重山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaeyama Shrine|1=Yaeyama Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391791}} (possibly {{ill|Ōishigaki Utaki|ja|大石垣御嶽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōishigaki Utaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}){{sfn|後田多敦ほか|2020|pp=25-28}} make up the five shrines{{sfn|加治順人|2018|pp=53-55}}.|group=Note}}, and a plan was drawn up to consolidate the more than 900 Utaki shrines into 60 village shrines and 150 subordinate shrines. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} Among the utaki that have been converted into shrines are {{ill|Shirogane-do|8=en|10=ja|11=白銀堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirogane-do|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Itoman, Okinawa|en|Itoman, Okinawa|WD=Q860662|lt=Itoman}}, {{ill|Tsukishiro-gu Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=月代宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukishiro-gu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Nanjō|en|Nanjō|WD=Q874691|lt=Sashiki}}, {{ill|Sashiki, Okinawa|en|Sashiki, Okinawa|WD=Q5119471|lt=Nanjō}},  {{ill|Sōkei-gu Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=惣慶宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōkei-gu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Awase Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=泡瀬神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awase Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ginoza, Okinawa|en|Ginoza, Okinawa|WD=Q1203012|lt=Ginoza village}}, {{ill|Shinzato-gu Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=新里宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinzato-gu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Motobu, Okinawa|en|Motobu, Okinawa|WD=Q1205928|lt=Motobu, Okinawa}},  {{ill|Toyama Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=十山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyama Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Yonaguni, Okinawa|en|Yonaguni, Okinawa|WD=Q702623|lt=Yonaguni town}}, and {{ill|Shirahama-jinja Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=白浜神社 (竹富町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirahama-jinja Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on {{ill|Iriomote Island|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iriomote Island|qq=}} . {{Sfn|加治順人|2018|qq=draft}} In addition, training seminars for {{ill|Noro (priestess)|en|Noro (priestess)|WD=Q11325791|lt=Noro}} recommended by local government leaders were held at Gokoku Shrines and other locations in an attempt to fill the shortage of priests by switching them from Noro to other priests. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} {{Refn|In contrast, {{ill|Yuta (shaman)|en|Yuta (shaman)|WD=Q11345595|lt=Yuta}} were being targeted for regulation during the same period for "misleading the public."{{sfn|加治順人|2018|pp=55-57}}。|group=Note}}
 However, the four-year plan, one village, one company, was thwarted by the worsening war situation. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} Furthermore, the Ministry of Home Affairs documents state that there were ten shrines in Okinawa as of 1938 (Showa 13) {{Sfn|影山正治|1972}}, but Sueyasu Daiko (1992) claims that by the end of the war the number of shrines was as high as 227 {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} .
Many shrines were destroyed and fell into disrepair following {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|the Battle of Okinawa|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Okinawa|qq=}} . However, it is not clear whether all of these were the result of U.S. military attacks. For example, in photographs taken by the U.S. military immediately after the Battle of Okinawa, Gokoku Shrine was still standing, although there was a large hole in the roof of the worship hall. However, Nagamine Ushio (1953) {{Refn|"The Past, Present, and Future of Gokoku Shrine," The Journey of Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine, pp. 119-128 (originally published in 1953)
|group=Note}}
 {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}
testified that "when I saw it in 1946, the torii gate was still there, but a few years later it was gone without a trace," suggesting that it may have been dismantled and used as building materials during the postwar reconstruction period. 
*

'''Toyama Shrine''' (十山神社, ''Tōyama Jinja'') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in the {{ill|Sonai (Yonaguni)|ja|祖納|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonai (Yonaguni)|1=Sonai (Yonaguni)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588548}} district of {{ill|Yonaguni, Okinawa|en|Yonaguni, Okinawa|WD=Q702623|lt=Yonaguni, Okinawa}}, {{ill|Yaeyama District, Okinawa|en|Yaeyama District, Okinawa|WD=Q629725|lt=Yaeyama District, Okinawa}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}}, Japan.

It is the westernmost shrine in Japan. The shrine is also known by its alternative name, '''Toyama Utaki''' (十山御嶽, ''Toyama Ugan'').

This shrine represents a unique fusion of {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|WD=Q812767|lt=Shinto}} and the {{ill|Ontake|en|Ontake|WD=Q11071176|lt=Utaki}} faith of the {{ill|Yaeyama Islands|ja|八重山諸島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaeyama Islands|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八重山列島|1=Yaeyama Islands|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q696496}}, maintaining distinctive traditions. The shrine’s annual {{ill|豊年祭|ja|豊年祭|1=Hōnensai|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/豊年祭|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōnensai|qq=}} (Harvest Festival) is held on {{ill|June 26 (Lunisolar)|lt=June 26 (Lunisolar)|lt=June 26 (lunar calendar)|ja|6月26日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 26 (lunar calendar)|qq=}} and includes a sacred {{ill|Tug of war|en|Tug of war|WD=Q102843|lt=Tug-of-war}} ritual.
==Excerpt from [[History of Shinto shrines in Okinawa]]==

=== Shrines during the war and the one village, one shrine concept ===
[[File:Miyako_harimizu_utaki.jpg|thumb|When {{ill|Miyako Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=漲水御嶽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyako Shrine|qq=}} was relocated to its current location in 1940, a combined ceremony was held and the shrine came to be called Hashimizu Shrine. {{Sfn|大城直樹|2022}}]]
During the same period, shrines closely related to war were also built. After the outbreak of {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|8=en|lt=the Russo-Japanese War|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Russo-Japanese War|qq=}} in 1904, prayers for victory began to be held repeatedly at Naminoue Shrine, and a ceremony to declare war on Japan in {{ill|World War I|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World War I|qq=}} was held there in 1914, and a ceremony to declare entry into the military was held there in 1917. In this situation, there was a demand for a shrine to commemorate those {{ill|Killed in action|en|Killed in action|WD=Q210392|lt=Killed in action}}, but the area surrounding {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|WD=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine}} was already a bustling shopping district, and it was recognized that it was not suitable as a place to worship the war dead. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} A new shrine was built in 1936 (Showa 11), and the shrine was renamed {{ill|Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=沖縄県護国神社|lt=Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} in 1940 (Showa 15). At the time of the name change, the shrine enshrined 310 deities in total, including one from the Sino-Japanese War and 195 from the Russo-Japanese War, making it a small shrine compared to {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=the Gokoku Shrines}} of other prefectures. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}

Okinawa is characterized by the fact that Gokoku Shrines dedicated to war dead were built by private citizens, and as of 2018, some still exist within the U.S. {{ill|Kadena Air Base|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadena Air Base|qq=}} and on {{ill|Miyako Island|en|Miyako Island|WD=Q700946|lt=Miyako Island}} . These are thought to have been enshrined at the Utaki shrine, and the rituals there are performed in the style of Ryukyu Shinto. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}

Also, since there were no infantry regiments in Okinawa Prefecture, being conscripted was the same as being sent to war outside the prefecture. It was known in Okinawa that on the mainland prayers for the military success of soldiers were offered at shrines, but in Okinawa in the early Showa period, there were only four functioning shrines with priests: {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|WD=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, [[Okinawa Shrine]], and {{ill|Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=沖縄県護国神社|lt=Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}. This raised the question of what to do in areas without shrines. As a result, six of the {{ill|Eight Ryukyu Shrines|ja|琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}}, which had fallen into disrepair, were quickly upgraded to prefectural or local shrines{{Efn|The six shrines are
* {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū|ja|安里八幡宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asato-Hachimangū|qq=}}
* {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}
* {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}
* {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}
* {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}
* {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}}}, and a plan was proposed to develop the local utaki shrines into shrines. This idea was first proposed around 1939, and was featured in a newspaper article on August 27, 1940 (Showa 15) as "One Village, One Company" {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} .The one village, one shrine concept was initially shelved due to lack of funds, but in 1943 the prefecture applied to the national government for grants to restore the [[eight Ryukyu shrines]]. In addition, five new shrines were designated as prefectural shrines {{Refn|{{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sefa-utaki|lt=Saiba Shrine|ja|斎場神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sefa-utaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Sefa-utaki|en|Sefa-utaki|WD=Q3087076|lt=Sefa-utaki}}), {{ill|Nakijin Castle|lt=Hokuzan Shrine|ja|北山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakijin Castle|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Nakijin Castle|en|Nakijin Castle|WD=Q508866|lt=Nakijin Castle}}), {{ill|Miyako shrines{{sfn|加治順人|2018|pp=53-55}}.|group=Note}}, and a plan was drawn up to consolidate the more than 900 Utaki shrines into 60 village shrines and 150 subordinate shrines. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} Among the Okinawa|WD=Q860662|lt=Itoman}}, {{ill|Tsukishiro-gu Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=月代宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukishiro-gu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Nanjō|en|Nanjō|WD=Q874691|lt=Sashiki}}, {{ill|Sashiki, Okinawa|en|Sashiki, Okinawa|WD=Q5119471|lt=Nanjō}},  {{ill|Sōkei-gu Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=惣慶宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōkei-gu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Awase Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=泡瀬神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awase Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ginoza, Okinawa|en|Ginoza, Okinawa|WD=Q1203012|lt=Ginoza village}}, {{ill|Shinzato-gu Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=新里宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinzato-gu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Motobu, Okinawa|en|Motobu, Okinawa|WD=Q1205928|lt=Motobu, Okinawa}},  {{ill|Toyama Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=十山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyama Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Yonaguni, Okinawa|en|Yonaguni, Okinawa|WD=Q702623|lt=Yonaguni town}}, and {{ill|Shirahama-jinja Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=白浜神社 (竹富町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirahama-jinja Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on {{ill|Iriomote Island|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iriomote Island|qq=}} . {{Sfn|加治順人|2018|qq=draft}} In addition, training seminars for {{ill|Noro (priestess)|en|Noro (priestess)|WD=Q11325791|lt=Noro}} recommended by local government leaders were held at Gokoku Shrines and other locations in an attempt to fill the shortage of priests by switching them from Noro to other priests. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} {{Refn|In contrast, {{ill|Yuta (shaman)|en|Yuta (shaman)|WD=Q11345595|lt=Yuta}} were being targeted for regulation during the same period for "misleading the public."{{sfn|加治順人|2018|pp=55-57}}。|group=Note}}
 However, the four-year plan, one village, one company, was thwarted by the worsening war situation. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} Furthermore, the Ministry of Home Affairs documents state that there were ten shrines in Okinawa as of 1938 (Showa 13) {{Sfn|影山正治|1972}}, but Sueyasu Daiko (1992) claims that by the end of the war the number of shrines was as high as 227 {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} .
Many shrines were destroyed and fell into disrepair following {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|the Battle of Okinawa|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Okinawa|qq=}} . However, it is not clear whether all of these were the result of U.S. military attacks. For example, in photographs taken by the U.S. military immediately after the Battle of Okinawa, Gokoku Shrine was still standing, although there was a large hole in the roof of the worship hall. However, Nagamine Ushio (1953) {{Refn|"The Past, Present, and Future of Gokoku Shrine," The Journey of Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine, pp. 119-128 (originally published in 1953)
|group=Note}}
 {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}
testified that "when I saw it in 1946, the torii gate was still there, but a few years later it was gone without a trace," suggesting that it may have been dismantled and used as building materials during the postwar reconstruction period. 
*

'''Toyama Shrine''' (十山神社, ''Tōyama Jinja'') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in the {{ill|Sonai (Yonaguni)|ja|祖納|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonai (Yonaguni)|1=Sonai (Yonaguni)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588548}} district of {{ill|Yonaguni, Okinawa|en|Yonaguni, Okinawa|WD=Q702623|lt=Yonaguni, Okinawa}}, {{ill|Yaeyama District, Okinawa|en|Yaeyama District, Okinawa|WD=Q629725|lt=Yaeyama District, Okinawa}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}}, Japan.

It is the westernmost shrine in Japan. The shrine is also known by its alternative name, '''Toyama Utaki''' (十山御嶽, ''Toyama Ugan'').

This shrine represents a unique fusion of {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|WD=Q812767|lt=Shinto}} and the {{ill|Ontake|en|Ontake|WD=Q11071176|lt=Utaki}} faith of the {{ill|Yaeyama Islands|ja|八重山諸島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaeyama Islands|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八重山列島|1=Yaeyama Islands|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q696496}}, maintaining distinctive traditions. The shrine’s annual {{ill|豊年祭|ja|豊年祭|1=Hōnensai|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/豊年祭|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōnensai|qq=}} (Harvest Festival) is held on {{ill|June 26 (Lunisolar)|lt=June 26 (Lunisolar)|lt=June 26 (lunar calendar)|ja|6月26日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 26 (lunar calendar)|qq=}} and includes a sacred {{ill|Tug of war|en|Tug of war|WD=Q102843|lt=Tug-of-war}} ritual.
==Excerpt from [[History of Shinto shrines in Okinawa]]==

=== Shrines during the war and the one village, one shrine concept ===
[[File:Miyako_harimizu_utaki.jpg|thumb|When {{ill|Miyako Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=漲水御嶽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyako Shrine|qq=}} was relocated to its current location in 1940, a combined ceremony was held and the shrine came to be called Hashimizu Shrine. {{Sfn|大城直樹|2022}}]]
During the same period, shrines closely related to war were also built. After the outbreak of {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|8=en|lt=the Russo-Japanese War|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Russo-Japanese War|qq=}} in 1904, prayers for victory began to be held repeatedly at Naminoue Shrine, and a ceremony to declare war on Japan in {{ill|World War I|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World War I|qq=}} was held there in 1914, and a ceremony to declare entry into the military was held there in 1917. In this situation, there was a demand for a shrine to commemorate those {{ill|Killed in action|en|Killed in action|WD=Q210392|lt=Killed in action}}, but the area surrounding {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|WD=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine}} was already a bustling shopping district, and it was recognized that it was not suitable as a place to worship the war dead. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} A new shrine was built in 1936 (Showa 11), and the shrine was renamed {{ill|Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=沖縄県護国神社|lt=Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} in 1940 (Showa 15). At the time of the name change, the shrine enshrined 310 deities in total, including one from the Sino-Japanese War and 195 from the Russo-Japanese War, making it a small shrine compared to {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=the Gokoku Shrines}} of other prefectures. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}

Okinawa is characterized by the fact that Gokoku Shrines dedicated to war dead were built by private citizens, and as of 2018, some still exist within the U.S. {{ill|Kadena Air Base|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadena Air Base|qq=}} and on {{ill|Miyako Island|en|Miyako Island|WD=Q700946|lt=Miyako Island}} . These are thought to have been enshrined at the Utaki shrine, and the rituals there are performed in the style of Ryukyu Shinto. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}

Also, since there were no infantry regiments in Okinawa Prefecture, being conscripted was the same as being sent to war outside the prefecture. It was known in Okinawa that on the mainland prayers for the military success of soldiers were offered at shrines, but in Okinawa in the early Showa period, there were only four functioning shrines with priests: {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|WD=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, [[Okinawa Shrine]], and {{ill|Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=沖縄県護国神社|lt=Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}. This raised the question of what to do in areas without shrines. As a result, six of the {{ill|Eight Ryukyu Shrines|ja|琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}}, which had fallen into disrepair, were quickly upgraded to prefectural or local shrines{{Efn|The six shrines are
* {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū|ja|安里八幡宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asato-Hachimangū|qq=}}
* {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}
* {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}
* {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}
* {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}
* {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}}}, and a plan was proposed to develop the local utaki shrines into shrines. This idea was first proposed around 1939, and was featured in a newspaper article on August 27, 1940 (Showa 15) as "One Village, One Company" {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} .The one village, one shrine concept was initially shelved due to lack of funds, but in 1943 the prefecture applied to the national government for grants to restore the [[eight Ryukyu shrines]]. In addition, five new shrines were designated as prefectural shrines {{Refn|{{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sefa-utaki|lt=Saiba Shrine|ja|斎場神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sefa-utaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Sefa-utaki|en|Sefa-utaki|WD=Q3087076|lt=Sefa-utaki}}), {{ill|Nakijin Castle|lt=Hokuzan Shrine|ja|北山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakijin Castle|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Nakijin Castle|en|Nakijin Castle|WD=Q508866|lt=Nakijin Castle}}), {{ill|Miyako Shrine|ja|宮古神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyako Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Yaeyama Shrine|ja|八重山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaeyama Shrine|1=Yaeyama Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391791}} (possibly {{ill|Ōishigaki Utaki|ja|大石垣御嶽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōishigaki Utaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}){{sfn|後田多敦ほか|2020|pp=25-28}} make up the five shrines{{sfn|加治順人|2018|pp=53-55}}.|group=Note}}, and a plan was drawn up to consolidate the more than 900 Utaki shrines into 60 village shrines and 150 subordinate shrines. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} Among the utaki that have been converted into shrines are {{ill|Shirogane-do|8=en|10=ja|11=白銀堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirogane-do|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Itoman, Okinawa|en|Itoman, Okinawa|WD=Q860662|lt=Itoman}}, {{ill|Tsukishiro-gu Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=月代宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukishiro-gu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Nanjō|en|Nanjō|WD=Q874691|lt=Sashiki}}, {{ill|Sashiki, Okinawa|en|Sashiki, Okinawa|WD=Q5119471|lt=Nanjō}},  {{ill|Sōkei-gu Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=惣慶宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōkei-gu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Awase Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=泡瀬神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awase Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ginoza, Okinawa|en|Ginoza, Okinawa|WD=Q1203012|lt=Ginoza village}}, {{ill|Shinzato-gu Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=新里宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinzato-gu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Motobu, Okinawa|en|Motobu, Okinawa|WD=Q1205928|lt=Motobu, Okinawa}},  {{ill|Toyama Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=十山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyama Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Yonaguni, Okinawa|en|Yonaguni, Okinawa|WD=Q702623|lt=Yonaguni town}}, and {{ill|Shirahama-jinja Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=白浜神社 (竹富町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirahama-jinja Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on {{ill|Iriomote Island|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iriomote Island|qq=}} . {{Sfn|加治順人|2018|qq=draft}} In addition, training seminars for {{ill|Noro (priestess)|en|Noro (priestess)|WD=Q11325791|lt=Noro}} recommended by local government leaders were held at Gokoku Shrines and other locations in an attempt to fill the shortage of priests by switching them from Noro to other priests. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} {{Refn|In contrast, {{ill|Yuta (shaman)|en|Yuta (shaman)|WD=Q11345595|lt=Yuta}} were being targeted for regulation during the same period for "misleading the public."{{sfn|加治順人|2018|pp=55-57}}。|group=Note}}
 However, the four-year plan, one village, one company, was thwarted by the worsening war situation. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} Furthermore, the Ministry of Home Affairs documents state that there were ten shrines in Okinawa as of 1938 (Showa 13) {{Sfn|影山正治|1972}}, but Sueyasu Daiko (1992) claims that by the end of the war the number of shrines was as high as 227 {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} .
Many shrines were destroyed and fell into disrepair following {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|the Battle of Okinawa|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Okinawa|qq=}} . However, it is not clear whether all of these were the result of U.S. military attacks. For example, in photographs taken by the U.S. military immediately after the Battle of Okinawa, Gokoku Shrine was still standing, although there was a large hole in the roof of the worship hall. However, Nagamine Ushio (1953) {{Refn|"The Past, Present, and Future of Gokoku Shrine," The Journey of Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine, pp. 119-128 (originally published in 1953)
|group=Note}}
 {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}
testified that "when I saw it in 1946, the torii gate was still there, but a few years later it was gone without a trace," suggesting that it may have been dismantled and used as building materials during the postwar reconstruction period.

'''Toyama Shrine''' (十山神社, ''Tōyama Jinja'') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in the {{ill|Sonai (Yonaguni)|ja|祖納|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonai (Yonaguni)|1=Sonai (Yonaguni)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588548}} district of {{ill|Yonaguni, Okinawa|en|Yonaguni, Okinawa|WD=Q702623|lt=Yonaguni, Okinawa}}, {{ill|Yaeyama District, Okinawa|en|Yaeyama District, Okinawa|WD=Q629725|lt=Yaeyama District, Okinawa}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}}, Japan.

It is the westernmost shrine in Japan. The shrine is also known by its alternative name, '''Toyama Utaki''' (十山御嶽, ''Toyama Ugan'').

This shrine represents a unique fusion of {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|WD=Q812767|lt=Shinto}} and the {{ill|Ontake|en|Ontake|WD=Q11071176|lt=Utaki}} faith of the {{ill|Yaeyama Islands|ja|八重山諸島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaeyama Islands|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八重山列島|1=Yaeyama Islands|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q696496}}, maintaining distinctive traditions. The shrine’s annual {{ill|豊年祭|ja|豊年祭|1=Hōnensai|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/豊年祭|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōnensai|qq=}} (Harvest Festival) is held on {{ill|June 26 (Lunisolar)|lt=June 26 (Lunisolar)|lt=June 26 (lunar calendar)|ja|6月26日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 26 (lunar calendar)|qq=}} and includes a sacred {{ill|Tug of war|en|Tug of war|WD=Q102843|lt=Tug-of-war}} ritual.
==Excerpt from [[History of Shinto shrines in Okinawa]]==

=== Shrines during the war and the one village, one shrine concept ===
[[File:Miyako_harimizu_utaki.jpg|thumb|When {{ill|Miyako Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=漲水御嶽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyako Shrine|qq=}} was relocated to its current location in 1940, a combined ceremony was held and the shrine came to be called Hashimizu Shrine. {{Sfn|大城直樹|2022}}]]
During the same period, shrines closely related to war were also built. After the outbreak of {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|8=en|lt=the Russo-Japanese War|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Russo-Japanese War|qq=}} in 1904, prayers for victory began to be held repeatedly at Naminoue Shrine, and a ceremony to declare war on Japan in {{ill|World War I|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World War I|qq=}} was held there in 1914, and a ceremony to declare entry into the military was held there in 1917. In this situation, there was a demand for a shrine to commemorate those {{ill|Killed in action|en|Killed in action|WD=Q210392|lt=Killed in action}}, but the area surrounding {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|WD=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine}} was already a bustling shopping district, and it was recognized that it was not suitable as a place to worship the war dead. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} A new shrine was built in 1936 (Showa 11), and the shrine was renamed {{ill|Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=沖縄県護国神社|lt=Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} in 1940 (Showa 15). At the time of the name change, the shrine enshrined 310 deities in total, including one from the Sino-Japanese War and 195 from the Russo-Japanese War, making it a small shrine compared to {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=the Gokoku Shrines}} of other prefectures. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}

Okinawa is characterized by the fact that Gokoku Shrines dedicated to war dead were built by private citizens, and as of 2018, some still exist within the U.S. {{ill|Kadena Air Base|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadena Air Base|qq=}} and on {{ill|Miyako Island|en|Miyako Island|WD=Q700946|lt=Miyako Island}} . These are thought to have been enshrined at the Utaki shrine, and the rituals there are performed in the style of Ryukyu Shinto. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}

Also, since there were no infantry regiments in Okinawa Prefecture, being conscripted was the same as being sent to war outside the prefecture. It was known in Okinawa that on the mainland prayers for the military success of soldiers were offered at shrines, but in Okinawa in the early Showa period, there were only four functioning shrines with priests: {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|WD=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, [[Okinawa Shrine]], and {{ill|Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=沖縄県護国神社|lt=Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}. This raised the question of what to do in areas without shrines. As a result, six of the {{ill|Eight Ryukyu Shrines|ja|琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}}, which had fallen into disrepair, were quickly upgraded to prefectural or local shrines{{Efn|The six shrines are
* {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū|ja|安里八幡宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asato-Hachimangū|qq=}}
* {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}
* {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}
* {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}
* {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}
* {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}}}, and a plan was proposed to develop the local utaki shrines into shrines. This idea was first proposed around 1939, and was featured in a newspaper article on August 27, 1940 (Showa 15) as "One Village, One Company" {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} .The one village, one shrine concept was initially shelved due to lack of funds, but in 1943 the prefecture applied to the national government for grants to restore the [[eight Ryukyu shrines]]. In addition, five new shrines were designated as prefectural shrines {{Refn|{{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sefa-utaki|lt=Saiba Shrine|ja|斎場神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sefa-utaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Sefa-utaki|en|Sefa-utaki|WD=Q3087076|lt=Sefa-utaki}}), {{ill|Nakijin Castle|lt=Hokuzan Shrine|ja|北山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakijin Castle|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Nakijin Castle|en|Nakijin Castle|WD=Q508866|lt=Nakijin Castle}}), {{ill|Miyako Shrine|ja|宮古神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyako Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Yaeyama Shrine|ja|八重山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaeyama Shrine|1=Yaeyama Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391791}} (possibly {{ill|Ōishigaki Utaki|ja|大石垣御嶽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōishigaki Utaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}){{sfn|後田多敦ほか|2020|pp=25-28}} make up the five shrines{{sfn|加治順人|2018|pp=53-55}}.|group=Note}}, and a plan was drawn up to consolidate the more than 900 Utaki shrines into 60 village shrines and 150 subordinate shrines. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} Among the utaki that have been converted into shrines are {{ill|Shirogane-do|8=en|10=ja|11=白銀堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirogane-do|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Itoman, Okinawa|en|Itoman, Okinawa|WD=Q860662|lt=Itoman}}, {{ill|Tsukishiro-gu Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=月代宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukishiro-gu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Nanjō|en|Nanjō|WD=Q874691|lt=Sashiki}}, {{ill|Sashiki, Okinawa|en|Sashiki, Okinawa|WD=Q5119471|lt=Nanjō}},  {{ill|Sōkei-gu Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=惣慶宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōkei-gu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Awase Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=泡瀬神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awase Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ginoza, Okinawa|en|Ginoza, Okinawa|WD=Q1203012|lt=Ginoza village}}, {{ill|Shinzato-gu Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=新里宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinzato-gu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Motobu, Okinawa|en|Motobu, Okinawa|WD=Q1205928|lt=Motobu, Okinawa}},  {{ill|Toyama Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=十山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyama Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Yonaguni, Okinawa|en|Yonaguni, Okinawa|WD=Q702623|lt=Yonaguni town}}, and {{ill|Shirahama-jinja Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=白浜神社 (竹富町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirahama-jinja Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on {{ill|Iriomote Island|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iriomote Island|qq=}} . {{Sfn|加治順人|2018|qq=draft}} In addition, training seminars for {{ill|Noro (priestess)|en|Noro (priestess)|WD=Q11325791|lt=Noro}} recommended by local government leaders were held at Gokoku Shrines and other locations in an attempt to fill the shortage of priests by switching them from Noro to other priests. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} {{Refn|In contrast, {{ill|Yuta (shaman)|en|Yuta (shaman)|WD=Q11345595|lt=Yuta}} were being targeted for regulation during the same period for "misleading the public."{{sfn|加治順人|2018|pp=55-57}}。|group=Note}}
 However, the four-year plan, one village, one company, was thwarted by the worsening war situation. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} Furthermore, the Ministry of Home Affairs documents state that there were ten shrines in Okinawa as of 1938 (Showa 13) {{Sfn|影山正治|1972}}, but Sueyasu Daiko (1992) claims that by the end of the war the number of shrines was as high as 227 {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} .
Many shrines were destroyed and fell into disrepair following {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|the Battle of Okinawa|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Okinawa|qq=}} . However, it is not clear whether all of these were the result of U.S. military attacks. For example, in photographs taken by the U.S. military immediately after the Battle of Okinawa, Gokoku Shrine was still standing, although there was a large hole in the roof of the worship hall. However, Nagamine Ushio (1953) {{Refn|"The Past, Present, and Future of Gokoku Shrine," The Journey of Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine, pp. 119-128 (originally published in 1953)
|group=Note}}
 {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}
testified that "when I saw it in 1946, the torii gate was still there, but a few years later it was gone without a trace," suggesting that it may have been dismantled and used as building materials during the postwar reconstruction period. 
*

|coordinates ={{Coord|24.469722|N|123.003528|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}

|map = Japan Okinawa
|location = {{ill|Yonaguni|WD=Q702623}}

'''Toyama Shrine''' (十山神社, ''Tōyama Jinja'') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in the {{ill|Sonai (Yonaguni)|ja|祖納|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonai (Yonaguni)|1=Sonai (Yonaguni)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588548}} district of {{ill|Yonaguni, Okinawa|en|Yonaguni, Okinawa|WD=Q702623|lt=Yonaguni, Okinawa}}, {{ill|Yaeyama District, Okinawa|en|Yaeyama District, Okinawa|WD=Q629725|lt=Yaeyama District, Okinawa}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}}, Japan.

It is the westernmost shrine in Japan. The shrine is also known by its alternative name, '''Toyama Utaki''' (十山御嶽, ''Toyama Ugan'').

This shrine represents a unique fusion of {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|WD=Q812767|lt=Shinto}} and the {{ill|Ontake|en|Ontake|WD=Q11071176|lt=Utaki}} faith of the {{ill|Yaeyama Islands|ja|八重山諸島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaeyama Islands|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八重山列島|1=Yaeyama Islands|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q696496}}, maintaining distinctive traditions. The shrine’s annual {{ill|豊年祭|ja|豊年祭|1=Hōnensai|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/豊年祭|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōnensai|qq=}} (Harvest Festival) is held on {{ill|June 26 (Lunisolar)|lt=June 26 (Lunisolar)|lt=June 26 (lunar calendar)|ja|6月26日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 26 (lunar calendar)|qq=}} and includes a sacred {{ill|Tug of war|en|Tug of war|WD=Q102843|lt=Tug-of-war}} ritual.
==Excerpt from [[History of Shinto shrines in Okinawa]]==

=== Shrines during the war and the one village, one shrine concept ===
[[File:Miyako_harimizu_utaki.jpg|thumb|When {{ill|Miyako Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=漲水御嶽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyako Shrine|qq=}} was relocated to its current location in 1940, a combined ceremony was held and the shrine came to be called Hashimizu Shrine. {{Sfn|大城直樹|2022}}]]
During the same period, shrines closely related to war were also built. After the outbreak of {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|8=en|lt=the Russo-Japanese War|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Russo-Japanese War|qq=}} in 1904, prayers for victory began to be held repeatedly at Naminoue Shrine, and a ceremony to declare war on Japan in {{ill|World War I|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World War I|qq=}} was held there in 1914, and a ceremony to declare entry into the military was held there in 1917. In this situation, there was a demand for a shrine to commemorate those {{ill|Killed in action|en|Killed in action|WD=Q210392|lt=Killed in action}}, but the area surrounding {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|WD=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine}} was already a bustling shopping district, and it was recognized that it was not suitable as a place to worship the war dead. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} A new shrine was built in 1936 (Showa 11), and the shrine was renamed {{ill|Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=沖縄県護国神社|lt=Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} in 1940 (Showa 15). At the time of the name change, the shrine enshrined 310 deities in total, including one from the Sino-Japanese War and 195 from the Russo-Japanese War, making it a small shrine compared to {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=the Gokoku Shrines}} of other prefectures. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}

Okinawa is characterized by the fact that Gokoku Shrines dedicated to war dead were built by private citizens, and as of 2018, some still exist within the U.S. {{ill|Kadena Air Base|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadena Air Base|qq=}} and on {{ill|Miyako Island|en|Miyako Island|WD=Q700946|lt=Miyako Island}} . These are thought to have been enshrined at the Utaki shrine, and the rituals there are performed in the style of Ryukyu Shinto. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}

Also, since there were no infantry regiments in Okinawa Prefecture, being conscripted was the same as being sent to war outside the prefecture. It was known in Okinawa that on the mainland prayers for the military success of soldiers were offered at shrines, but in Okinawa in the early Showa period, there were only four functioning shrines with priests: {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|WD=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, [[Okinawa Shrine]], and {{ill|Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=沖縄県護国神社|lt=Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}. This raised the question of what to do in areas without shrines. As a result, six of the {{ill|Eight Ryukyu Shrines|ja|琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}}, which had fallen into disrepair, were quickly upgraded to prefectural or local shrines{{Efn|The six shrines are
* {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū|ja|安里八幡宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asato-Hachimangū|qq=}}
* {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}
* {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}
* {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}
* {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}
* {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}}}, and a plan was proposed to develop the local utaki shrines into shrines. This idea was first proposed around 1939, and was featured in a newspaper article on August 27, 1940 (Showa 15) as "One Village, One Company" {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} .The one village, one shrine concept was initially shelved due to lack of funds, but in 1943 the prefecture applied to the national government for grants to restore the [[eight Ryukyu shrines]]. In addition, five new shrines were designated as prefectural shrines {{Refn|{{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sefa-utaki|lt=Saiba Shrine|ja|斎場神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sefa-utaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Sefa-utaki|en|Sefa-utaki|WD=Q3087076|lt=Sefa-utaki}}), {{ill|Nakijin Castle|lt=Hokuzan Shrine|ja|北山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakijin Castle|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Nakijin Castle|en|Nakijin Castle|WD=Q508866|lt=Nakijin Castle}}), {{ill|Miyako Shrine|ja|宮古神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyako Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Yaeyama Shrine|ja|八重山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaeyama Shrine|1=Yaeyama Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391791}} (possibly {{ill|Ōishigaki Utaki|ja|大石垣御嶽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōishigaki Utaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}){{sfn|後田多敦ほか|2020|pp=25-28}} make up the five shrines{{sfn|加治順人|2018|pp=53-55}}.|group=Note}}, and a plan was drawn up to consolidate the more than 900 Utaki shrines into 60 village shrines and 150 subordinate shrines. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} Among the utaki that have been converted into shrines are {{ill|Shirogane-do|8=en|10=ja|11=白銀堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirogane-do|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Itoman, Okinawa|en|Itoman, Okinawa|WD=Q860662|lt=Itoman}}, {{ill|Tsukishiro-gu Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=月代宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukishiro-gu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Nanjō|en|Nanjō|WD=Q874691|lt=Sashiki}}, {{ill|Sashiki, Okinawa|en|Sashiki, Okinawa|WD=Q5119471|lt=Nanjō}},  {{ill|Sōkei-gu Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=惣慶宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōkei-gu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Awase Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=泡瀬神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awase Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ginoza, Okinawa|en|Ginoza, Okinawa|WD=Q1203012|lt=Ginoza village}}, {{ill|Shinzato-gu Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=新里宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinzato-gu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Motobu, Okinawa|en|Motobu, Okinawa|WD=Q1205928|lt=Motobu, Okinawa}},  {{ill|Toyama Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=十山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyama Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Yonaguni, Okinawa|en|Yonaguni, Okinawa|WD=Q702623|lt=Yonaguni town}}, and {{ill|Shirahama-jinja Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=白浜神社 (竹富町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirahama-jinja Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on {{ill|Iriomote Island|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iriomote Island|qq=}} . {{Sfn|加治順人|2018|qq=draft}} In addition, training seminars for {{ill|Noro (priestess)|en|Noro (priestess)|WD=Q11325791|lt=Noro}} recommended by local government leaders were held at Gokoku Shrines and other locations in an attempt to fill the shortage of priests by switching them from Noro to other priests. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} {{Refn|In contrast, {{ill|Yuta (shaman)|en|Yuta (shaman)|WD=Q11345595|lt=Yuta}} were being targeted for regulation during the same period for "misleading the public."{{sfn|加治順人|2018|pp=55-57}}。|group=Note}}
 However, the four-year plan, one village, one company, was thwarted by the worsening war situation. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} Furthermore, the Ministry of Home Affairs documents state that there were ten shrines in Okinawa as of 1938 (Showa 13) {{Sfn|影山正治|1972}}, but Sueyasu Daiko (1992) claims that by the end of the war the number of shrines was as high as 227 {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} .
Many shrines were destroyed and fell into disrepair following {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|the Battle of Okinawa|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Okinawa|qq=}} . However, it is not clear whether all of these were the result of U.S. military attacks. For example, in photographs taken by the U.S. military immediately after the Battle of Okinawa, Gokoku Shrine was still standing, although there was a large hole in the roof of the worship hall. However, Nagamine Ushio (1953) {{Refn|"The Past, Present, and Future of Gokoku Shrine," The Journey of Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine, pp. 119-128 (originally published in 1953)
|group=Note}}
 {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}
testified that "when I saw it in 1946, the torii gate was still there, but a few years later it was gone without a trace," suggesting that it may have been dismantled and used as building materials during the postwar reconstruction period.

'''Toyama Shrine''' (十山神社, ''Tōyama Jinja'') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in the {{ill|Sonai (Yonaguni)|ja|祖納|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonai (Yonaguni)|1=Q11588548|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588548}} district of {{ill|Yonaguni, Okinawa|en|Yonaguni, Okinawa|WD=Q702623|lt=Yonaguni, Okinawa|1=Yonaguni, Okinawa}}, {{ill|Yaeyama District, Okinawa|en|Yaeyama District, Okinawa|WD=Q629725|lt=Yaeyama District, Okinawa|1=Yaeyama District, Okinawa}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture|1=Okinawa Prefecture}}, Japan.

It is the westernmost shrine in Japan. The shrine is also known by its alternative name, '''Toyama Utaki''' (十山御嶽, ''Toyama Ugan'').

This shrine represents a unique fusion of {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|WD=Q812767|lt=Shinto|1=Shinto}} and the {{ill|Ontake|en|Ontake|WD=Q11071176|lt=Utaki|1=Ontake}} faith of the {{ill|Yaeyama Islands|ja|八重山諸島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaeyama Islands|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八重山列島|1=Yaeyama Islands|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q696496}}, maintaining distinctive traditions. The shrine’s annual {{ill|豊年祭|ja|豊年祭|1=Hōnensai|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/豊年祭|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōnensai|qq=}} (Harvest Festival) is held on {{ill|June 26 (Lunisolar)|lt=June 26 (Lunisolar)|lt=June 26 (lunar calendar)|ja|6月26日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 26 (lunar calendar)|qq=}} and includes a sacred {{ill|Tug of war|en|Tug of war|WD=Q102843|lt=Tug-of-war|1=Tug of war}} ritual.
==Excerpt from [[History of Shinto shrines in Okinawa]]==

=== Shrines during the war and the one village, one shrine concept ===
[[File:Miyako_harimizu_utaki.jpg|thumb|When {{ill|Miyako Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=漲水御嶽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyako Shrine|qq=}} was relocated to its current location in 1940, a combined ceremony was held and the shrine came to be called Hashimizu Shrine. {{Sfn|大城直樹|2022}}]]
During the same period, shrines closely related to war were also built. After the outbreak of {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|8=en|lt=the Russo-Japanese War|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Russo-Japanese War|qq=}} in 1904, prayers for victory began to be held repeatedly at Naminoue Shrine, and a ceremony to declare war on Japan in {{ill|World War I|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World War I|qq=}} was held there in 1914, and a ceremony to declare entry into the military was held there in 1917. In this situation, there was a demand for a shrine to commemorate those {{ill|Killed in action|en|Killed in action|WD=Q210392|lt=Killed in action|1=Killed in action}}, but the area surrounding {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|WD=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine|1=Naminoue Shrine}} was already a bustling shopping district, and it was recognized that it was not suitable as a place to worship the war dead. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} A new shrine was built in 1936 (Showa 11), and the shrine was renamed {{ill|Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=沖縄県護国神社|lt=Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} in 1940 (Showa 15). At the time of the name change, the shrine enshrined 310 deities in total, including one from the Sino-Japanese War and 195 from the Russo-Japanese War, making it a small shrine compared to {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=the Gokoku Shrines|1=Gokoku Shrines}} of other prefectures. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}

Okinawa is characterized by the fact that Gokoku Shrines dedicated to war dead were built by private citizens, and as of 2018, some still exist within the U.S. {{ill|Kadena Air Base|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadena Air Base|qq=}} and on {{ill|Miyako Island|en|Miyako Island|WD=Q700946|lt=Miyako Island|1=Miyako Island}} . These are thought to have been enshrined at the Utaki shrine, and the rituals there are performed in the style of Ryukyu Shinto. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}

Also, since there were no infantry regiments in Okinawa Prefecture, being conscripted was the same as being sent to war outside the prefecture. It was known in Okinawa that on the mainland prayers for the military success of soldiers were offered at shrines, but in Okinawa in the early Showa period, there were only four functioning shrines with priests: {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|WD=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine|1=Naminoue Shrine}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, [[Okinawa Shrine]], and {{ill|Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=沖縄県護国神社|lt=Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}. This raised the question of what to do in areas without shrines. As a result, six of the {{ill|Eight Ryukyu Shrines|ja|琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}}, which had fallen into disrepair, were quickly upgraded to prefectural or local shrines{{Efn|The six shrines are
* {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū|ja|安里八幡宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asato-Hachimangū|qq=}}
* {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}
* {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}
* {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}
* {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}
* {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}}}, and a plan was proposed to develop the local utaki shrines into shrines. This idea was first proposed around 1939, and was featured in a newspaper article on August 27, 1940 (Showa 15) as "One Village, One Company" {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} .The one village, one shrine concept was initially shelved due to lack of funds, but in 1943 the prefecture applied to the national government for grants to restore the [[eight Ryukyu shrines]]. In addition, five new shrines were designated as prefectural shrines {{Refn|{{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sefa-utaki|lt=Saiba Shrine|ja|斎場神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sefa-utaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Sefa-utaki|en|Sefa-utaki|WD=Q3087076|lt=Sefa-utaki|1=Sefa-utaki}}), {{ill|Nakijin Castle|lt=Hokuzan Shrine|ja|北山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakijin Castle|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Nakijin Castle|en|Nakijin Castle|WD=Q508866|lt=Nakijin Castle|1=Nakijin Castle}}), {{ill|Miyako Shrine|ja|宮古神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyako Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Yaeyama Shrine|ja|八重山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaeyama Shrine|1=Yaeyama Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391791}} (possibly {{ill|Ōishigaki Utaki|ja|大石垣御嶽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōishigaki Utaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}){{sfn|後田多敦ほか|2020|pp=25-28}} make up the five shrines{{sfn|加治順人|2018|pp=53-55}}.|group=Note}}, and a plan was drawn up to consolidate the more than 900 Utaki shrines into 60 village shrines and 150 subordinate shrines. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} Among the utaki that have been converted into shrines are {{ill|Shirogane-do|8=en|10=ja|11=白銀堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirogane-do|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Itoman, Okinawa|en|Itoman, Okinawa|WD=Q860662|lt=Itoman|1=Itoman, Okinawa}}, {{ill|Tsukishiro-gu Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=月代宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukishiro-gu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Nanjō|en|Nanjō|WD=Q874691|lt=Sashiki|1=Nanjō}}, {{ill|Sashiki, Okinawa|en|Sashiki, Okinawa|WD=Q5119471|lt=Nanjō|1=Sashiki, Okinawa}},  {{ill|Sōkei-gu Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=惣慶宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōkei-gu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Awase Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=泡瀬神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awase Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ginoza, Okinawa|en|Ginoza, Okinawa|WD=Q1203012|lt=Ginoza village|1=Ginoza, Okinawa}}, {{ill|Shinzato-gu Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=新里宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinzato-gu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Motobu, Okinawa|en|Motobu, Okinawa|WD=Q1205928|lt=Motobu, Okinawa|1=Motobu, Okinawa}},  {{ill|Toyama Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=十山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyama Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Yonaguni, Okinawa|en|Yonaguni, Okinawa|WD=Q702623|lt=Yonaguni town|1=Yonaguni, Okinawa}}, and {{ill|Shirahama-jinja Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=白浜神社 (竹富町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirahama-jinja Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on {{ill|Iriomote Island|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iriomote Island|qq=}} . {{Sfn|加治順人|2018|qq=draft}} In addition, training seminars for {{ill|Noro (priestess)|en|Noro (priestess)|WD=Q11325791|lt=Noro|1=Noro (priestess)}} recommended by local government leaders were held at Gokoku Shrines and other locations in an attempt to fill the shortage of priests by switching them from Noro to other priests. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} {{Refn|In contrast, {{ill|Yuta (shaman)|en|Yuta (shaman)|WD=Q11345595|lt=Yuta|1=Yuta (shaman)}} were being targeted for regulation during the same period for "misleading the public."{{sfn|加治順人|2018|pp=55-57}}。|group=Note}}
 However, the four-year plan, one village, one company, was thwarted by the worsening war situation. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} Furthermore, the Ministry of Home Affairs documents state that there were ten shrines in Okinawa as of 1938 (Showa 13) {{Sfn|影山正治|1972}}, but Sueyasu Daiko (1992) claims that by the end of the war the number of shrines was as high as 227 {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} .
Many shrines were destroyed and fell into disrepair following {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|the Battle of Okinawa|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Okinawa|qq=}} . However, it is not clear whether all of these were the result of U.S. military attacks. For example, in photographs taken by the U.S. military immediately after the Battle of Okinawa, Gokoku Shrine was still standing, although there was a large hole in the roof of the worship hall. However, Nagamine Ushio (1953) {{Refn|"The Past, Present, and Future of Gokoku Shrine," The Journey of Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine, pp. 119-128 (originally published in 1953)
|group=Note}}
 {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}
testified that "when I saw it in 1946, the torii gate was still there, but a few years later it was gone without a trace," suggesting that it may have been dismantled and used as building materials during the postwar reconstruction period. 
*

'''Toyama Shrine''' (十山神社, ''Tōyama Jinja'') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in the {{ill|Sonai (Yonaguni)|ja|祖納|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonai (Yonaguni)|1=Q11588548|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588548}} district of {{ill|Yonaguni, Okinawa|en|Yonaguni, Okinawa|WD=Q702623|lt=Yonaguni, Okinawa|1=Yonaguni, Okinawa}}, {{ill|Yaeyama District, Okinawa|en|Yaeyama District, Okinawa|WD=Q629725|lt=Yaeyama District, Okinawa|1=Yaeyama District, Okinawa}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture|1=Okinawa Prefecture}}, Japan.

It is the westernmost shrine in Japan. The shrine is also known by its alternative name, '''Toyama Utaki''' (十山御嶽, ''Toyama Ugan'').

This shrine represents a unique fusion of {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|WD=Q812767|lt=Shinto|1=Shinto}} and the {{ill|Ontake|en|Ontake|WD=Q11071176|lt=Utaki|1=Ontake}} faith of the {{ill|Yaeyama Islands|ja|八重山諸島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaeyama Islands|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八重山列島|1=Yaeyama Islands|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q696496}}, maintaining distinctive traditions. The shrine’s annual {{ill|豊年祭|ja|豊年祭|1=Hōnensai|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/豊年祭|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōnensai|qq=}} (Harvest Festival) is held on {{ill|June 26 (Lunisolar)|lt=June 26 (Lunisolar)|lt=June 26 (lunar calendar)|ja|6月26日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 26 (lunar calendar)|qq=}} and includes a sacred {{ill|Tug of war|en|Tug of war|WD=Q102843|lt=Tug-of-war|1=Tug of war}} ritual.
==Excerpt from [[History of Shinto shrines in Okinawa]]==

=== Shrines during the war and the one village, one shrine concept ===
[[File:Miyako_harimizu_utaki.jpg|thumb|When {{ill|Miyako Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=漲水御嶽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyako Shrine|qq=}} was relocated to its current location in 1940, a combined ceremony was held and the shrine came to be called Hashimizu Shrine. {{Sfn|大城直樹|2022}}]]
During the same period, shrines closely related to war were also built. After the outbreak of {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|8=en|lt=the Russo-Japanese War|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Russo-Japanese War|qq=}} in 1904, prayers for victory began to be held repeatedly at Naminoue Shrine, and a ceremony to declare war on Japan in {{ill|World War I|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World War I|qq=}} was held there in 1914, and a ceremony to declare entry into the military was held there in 1917. In this situation, there was a demand for a shrine to commemorate those {{ill|Killed in action|en|Killed in action|WD=Q210392|lt=Killed in action|1=Killed in action}}, but the area surrounding {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|WD=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine|1=Naminoue Shrine}} was already a bustling shopping district, and it was recognized that it was not suitable as a place to worship the war dead. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} A new shrine was built in 1936 (Showa 11), and the shrine was renamed {{ill|Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=沖縄県護国神社|lt=Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} in 1940 (Showa 15). At the time of the name change, the shrine enshrined 310 deities in total, including one from the Sino-Japanese War and 195 from the Russo-Japanese War, making it a small shrine compared to {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=the Gokoku Shrines|1=Gokoku Shrines}} of other prefectures. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}

Okinawa is characterized by the fact that Gokoku Shrines dedicated to war dead were built by private citizens, and as of 2018, some still exist within the U.S. {{ill|Kadena Air Base|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadena Air Base|qq=}} and on {{ill|Miyako Island|en|Miyako Island|WD=Q700946|lt=Miyako Island|1=Miyako Island}} . These are thought to have been enshrined at the Utaki shrine, and the rituals there are performed in the style of Ryukyu Shinto. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}

Also, since there were no infantry regiments in Okinawa Prefecture, being conscripted was the same as being sent to war outside the prefecture. It was known in Okinawa that on the mainland prayers for the military success of soldiers were offered at shrines, but in Okinawa in the early Showa period, there were only four functioning shrines with priests: {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|WD=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine|1=Naminoue Shrine}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, [[Okinawa Shrine]], and {{ill|Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=沖縄県護国神社|lt=Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}. This raised the question of what to do in areas without shrines. As a result, six of the {{ill|Eight Ryukyu Shrines|ja|琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}}, which had fallen into disrepair, were quickly upgraded to prefectural or local shrines{{Efn|The six shrines are
* {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū|ja|安里八幡宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asato-Hachimangū|qq=}}
* {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}
* {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}
* {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}
* {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}
* {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}}}, and a plan was proposed to develop the local utaki shrines into shrines. This idea was first proposed around 1939, and was featured in a newspaper article on August 27, 1940 (Showa 15) as "One Village, One Company" {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} .The one village, one shrine concept was initially shelved due to lack of funds, but in 1943 the prefecture applied to the national government for grants to restore the [[eight Ryukyu shrines]]. In addition, five new shrines were designated as prefectural shrines {{Refn|{{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sefa-utaki|lt=Saiba and {{ill|Shirahama-jinja Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=白浜神社 (竹富町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirahama-jinja Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on {{ill|Iriomote Island|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iriomote Island|qq=}} . {{Sfn|加治順人|2018|qq=draft}} In addition, training seminars for {{ill|Noro (priestess)|en|Noro (priestess)|WD=Q11325791|lt=Noro|1=Noro (priestess)}} recommended by local government leaders were held at Gokoku Shrines and other locations in an attempt to fill the shortage of priests by switching them from Noro to other priests. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} {{Refn|In contrast, {{ill|Yuta (shaman)|en|Yuta (shaman)|WD=Q11345595|lt=Yuta|1=Yuta (shaman)}} were being targeted for regulation during the same period for "misleading the public."{{sfn|加治順人|2018|pp=55-57}}。|group=Note}}
 However, the four-year plan, one village, one company, was thwarted by the worsening war situation. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} Furthermore, the Ministry of Home Affairs documents state that there were ten shrines in Okinawa as of 1938 (Showa 13) {{Sfn|影山正治|1972}}, but Sueyasu Daiko (1992) claims that by the end of the war the number of shrines was as high as 227 {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} .
Many shrines were destroyed and fell into disrepair following {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|the Battle of Okinawa|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Okinawa|qq=}} . However, it is not clear whether all of these were the result of U.S. military attacks. For example, in photographs taken by the U.S. military immediately after the Battle of Okinawa, Gokoku Shrine was still standing, although there was a large hole in the roof of the worship hall. However, Nagamine Ushio (1953) {{Refn|"The Past, Present, and Future of Gokoku Shrine," The Journey of Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine, pp. 119-128 (originally published in 1953)
|group=Note}}
 {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}
testified that "when I saw it in 1946, the torii gate was still there, but a few years later it was gone without a trace," suggesting that it may have been dismantled and used as building materials during the postwar reconstruction period. 
*
